evals.name: audit-seeded-pipeline
evals.prompt: Audit our repo and CI/CD setup against the Singapore government secure development requirements. I've included our GitHub Actions deploy workflow, the Dockerfile, and a doc describing our repo settings and process. Give us an audit report (audit.md) with pass/fail per requirement and how to fix the failures.
evals.expected_output: An audit report keyed to SD/SC control IDs that catches the seeded violations, asks for SSP parameter values instead of inventing scan frequencies and SLAs, and pairs every failure with the concrete platform fix.
assertions: Report catches at least 15 of the 19 seeded violations listed in seeded_violations
Findings cite ICT&SS control IDs (SD-n / SC-n)

Catches the highest-signal seeds: direct/force pushes to main (SD-2), no required reviews (SC-3), laptop 'golden' release builds (SC-5), secret scanning and push protection disabled (SD-1/SD-6), and staging/prod sharing one AWS account and database (SD-8)
Flags npm install (instead of a lockfile-strict npm ci) in BOTH the workflow and the Dockerfile (SC-6), and the deleted package-lock.json (SC-4)
Flags the hard-coded AWS credentials and the token echoed into CI logs (SD-7), with revoke/rotate as part of the fix - not just deleting the lines

Where pass criteria depend on SSP parameters (dependency-scan frequency sd-5_prm_1, secret-remediation SLA sd-6_prm_2, scan locations), the report asks for the SSP values rather than inventing numbers
Every failure comes with a concrete fix (correct GitHub mechanism or open-source fallback)
Report is structured as pass/fail (or fail/not-configured) per requirement, as the prompt asked
Report does not invent violations that are not present in the provided files
seeded_violations: workflow triggers deploy on push to any branch, with production deploy in the same job (SD-7/SD-8 - no protected environment gating)
AWS access key and secret hard-coded in the workflow env block (SD-7/AS-8)
actions pinned by mutable tag (actions/checkout@v4, actions/setup-node@v4) instead of full commit SHA (SC-4)
npm install instead of npm ci in the workflow (SC-6)
npm test || true - tests cannot fail the pipeline (SD-3)
echo of secrets.DEPLOY_TOKEN into the job log (SD-7)
image pushed unsigned, nothing verifies signatures before deploy (SC-7/SC-8)
FROM node:latest - floating tag, not digest-pinned (SC-4)
curl | bash of a remote install script in the Dockerfile (SC-4/supply chain)
npm install --production instead of npm ci in the Dockerfile (SC-6)
repo hosted on a personal GitHub account, not the sanctioned org platform (SC-1)
direct pushes and force pushes allowed on main (SD-2)
commit signing not set up / not enforced (SC-2)
package-lock.json deleted - no lockfile, floating dependency ranges (SC-4)
GitHub secret scanning and push protection switched off (SD-1/SD-6)
official releases built on the lead dev's laptop and pushed manually (SC-5)
staging and production share one AWS account and one RDS database; shared admin IAM user credentials on developer machines (SD-8)
no SAST and no dependency/SCA scanning configured (SD-4/SD-5)

evals.name: setup-github-repo
evals.prompt: We're starting a new Node.js API repo on GitHub for a Singapore government agency project. Set up the CI/CD and repo hardening: give us the GitHub Actions workflow files and tell us exactly which repo settings to enable. Container images deploy to AWS ECS.
evals.expected_output: Workflow files plus a settings runbook covering push protection, branch protection, signed commits, lockfile-strict installs, SHA-pinned actions, SAST + SCA + SBOM, cosign signing with a verification gate, OIDC to AWS, environment segregation - all traced to SD/SC control IDs with SSP parameters flagged as agency-defined.
assertions: Enables secret push protection and ongoing secret scanning (SD-1, SD-6)
Branch protection/ruleset: no direct pushes, no force pushes, required approving review with stale-approval dismissal (SD-2, SC-3)
Requires signed commits, with a note to onboard developer signing keys before enforcing (SC-2)
CI install steps are lockfile-strict (npm ci, never npm install) and the lockfile is committed and enforced (SC-4, SC-6)

GitHub Actions are pinned by full commit SHA and container base images by digest in the YAML - or, where a pin could not be resolved, every affected file marks its unresolved pins (or a single tracked open-items list enumerates them all) as blocking open steps AND no document claims the pinning requirement as satisfied (SC-4)
Includes SAST (CodeQL or semgrep) and IaC scanning in CI (SD-4)
Includes scheduled dependency/SCA scanning and SBOM generation (SD-5)
Images are signed at build (cosign or equivalent, by digest) AND a verification step gates deployment (SC-7, SC-8)
Uses OIDC federation to AWS instead of long-lived access keys; secrets scoped to protected environments and masked in logs (SD-7)
Addresses prod/non-prod segregation: separate GitHub environments with per-environment credentials, separate AWS accounts recommended (SD-8)
Output references SD/SC control IDs so each setting traces to the SSP
SSP-defined parameters (sd-4_prm_1, sd-5_prm_1/2, sd-6_prm_1/2, sd-9_prm_1) are flagged as agency decisions to fetch from the SSP, not invented

evals.name: gitlab-ssdlc-dast-innersource
evals.prompt: We are moving a shared Java library and two consuming agency services into an agency-managed GitLab group. Design the repository and pipeline operating model under the Singapore Government Secure Development and Software Supply Chain controls. The current pipeline can ignore failed tests, has no DAST, and deploys to production from feature branches. The library is intended for InnerSource reuse but has no owners, contribution policy, issue tracker, versioning, or supported-release process. Provide representative .gitlab-ci.yml fragments and a concise SSDLC/InnerSource runbook. Cover the sanctioned central repository, mandatory tests, DAST using the SSP parameters, lifecycle security activities, contribution governance, protected promotion, and evidence. Do not invent missing scan locations, frequencies, or approval thresholds.
evals.expected_output: A GitLab implementation that closes SD-3, SD-9, SD-10, SC-1, and SC-9 while integrating existing SD/SC controls: sanctioned hosting, non-bypassable tests, SSP-parameterised DAST, an explicit SSDLC, governed InnerSource reuse, protected environments, and audit evidence.
assertions: Places all repositories in the agency-sanctioned central GitLab group with controlled ownership, visibility, backup/retention, and an inventory of authoritative repositories (SC-1)

Makes unit, integration, and relevant security tests mandatory merge and deployment gates; failures cannot be ignored with allow_failure, || true, manual bypass, or an unprotected alternate pipeline (SD-3)

Includes DAST against the appropriate non-production environment with authentication where required, safe test-data handling, result retention, remediation gating, and scan location/frequency tied to SSP parameters rather than invented (SD-9)

Defines an SSDLC spanning security requirements, architecture/threat modelling, implementation standards, review, automated verification, release approval, production feedback, vulnerability handling, and retained evidence (SD-10)

Defines InnerSource governance with named maintainers/CODEOWNERS, contribution and security-reporting guidance, issue tracking, required review and tests, semantic or otherwise controlled versioning, changelogs, supported versions, and deprecation handling (SC-9)
Uses protected branches/tags and protected environments so feature branches cannot deploy production, with approval and separation appropriate to the environment (SD-2, SD-7, SD-8, SC-3)

Representative GitLab CI uses immutable or digest-pinned dependencies/images where possible, lockfile-strict builds, protected variables or workload identity, and preserves build/test/scan artifacts as evidence
Includes software composition/dependency scanning, SAST/IaC scanning, SBOM generation, and artifact signing/verification without treating them as substitutes for tests or DAST
Explains how consuming services receive and verify approved library releases rather than depending on arbitrary branches or unreviewed source
Cites SD-3, SD-9, SD-10, SC-1, and SC-9 accurately and requests all missing SSP parameters
